diagrams.title: Memory Hierarchy and Register Relationship
diagrams.description: A layered pyramid diagram showing the relationship between CPU registers, cache, RAM, and ROM. It illustrates the storage trade-offs between speed, size, and volatility. Registers are at the top (fastest, smallest capacity), followed by cache, then RAM, then ROM/secondary storage at the bottom (slowest, largest capacity). Arrows indicate data flow direction.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the memory hierarchy concept and how registers, RAM, and ROM differ in speed, size, and data persistence characteristics

diagrams.title: RAM Internal Architecture
diagrams.description: A schematic diagram showing the internal structure of RAM memory. It displays a grid of memory cells organized in rows and columns, with row address decoder and column address decoder. Shows data lines (bit lines), word lines, and the read/write control circuit. Demonstrates how a specific memory location is accessed using row and column addresses.
diagrams.purpose: Explains how RAM internally organizes and accesses data through addressing, showing the volatile nature of RAM (capacitor-based storage)

diagrams.title: CPU Register Set and Data Flow
diagrams.description: A diagram showing the major CPU registers and how they interact with memory (RAM/ROM). Shows key registers: Program Counter (PC), Instruction Register (IR), Accumulator (ACC), Memory Address Register (MAR), Memory Data Register (MDR), and General Purpose Registers. Arrows show data movement between registers, ALU, and memory bus.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the role of different registers in instruction execution and how data flows between CPU registers and memory (RAM/ROM)
